calendars.name: Shire Reckoning
calendars.users: the Hobbits of the Shire (and, with other month-names, Bree)

week.note: Highday (Friday) was the chief day, of holiday after noon and evening feasts; 'Saturday thus corresponds more nearly to our Monday, and Thursday to our Saturday.' No month began on a Friday — hence the jest 'on Friday the first of Summerfilth' for a day that does not exist.

special.note: first day of the year — a Sterday, always
first Litheday
no weekday name; intended to correspond as nearly as possible to the summer solstice
leap-years only — the 184th day, a day of special merrymaking (occurred in 1420)
second Litheday (185th in leap-years)
last day of the year — a Highday, always

calendars.leap: Every fourth year, except in the last year of the century (S.R. centuries: Year 1 = T.A. 1601), there were four Lithedays: the Overlithe follows Mid-year's Day. [C]
calendars.epoch: Year 1 = the colonization of the Shire = T.A. 1601. Bree counted from T.A. 1300.
calendars.reform: Shire-reform (time of Isengrim II): Mid-year's Day and the Overlithe belong to no week, so every year begins on Sterday and every date keeps the same weekday for ever.
notes: Yuletide in full was six days — the last three and first three days of each year.
The Yuledays and Lithedays remained outside the months: January 1 (Afteryule 1) is the SECOND day of the year.

Month-names descend from the Men of the vales of Anduin; 'similar names were found in Dale and Rohan.' Solmath was pronounced Somath; Thrimidge archaically Thrimilch; Blotmath as Blodmath or Blommath.

calendars.name: Kings' Reckoning
calendars.era: Númenor from S.A. 1; Arnor & Gondor until T.A. 2059
calendars.users: the Númenóreans and the Realms in Exile 'until the end of the kings'

week.note: Seven days: the Eldarin six retained in dedication and order, the Two Trees' day altered to Aldëa (the White Tree), and a Sea-day, Eärenya, inserted after the Heavens' day.

special.note: first day, belonging to no month; mid-winter
mid-year's day (the 183rd)
last day, belonging to no month

calendars.leap: In every fourth year, except the last of a century (haranyë), two enderi replaced the loëndë. Millennial Deficit (4 h 46 m 40 s) added back in S.A. 1000, 2000, 3000; in exile the additions came 441 years late (T.A. 1000, 2000). [C]
calendars.epoch: Calculation began with S.A. 1. S.A. 3442 became T.A. 1; T.A. 4 was made a leap year instead of T.A. 3.
notes: The year had 365 days: ten months of 30 and two of 31 (Nárië and Cermië, either side of mid-year).
The day ran from sunrise (out of the eastern sea) to sunrise.
The year began in mid-winter, as among the Men of the North-west of the First Age.

calendars.name: Stewards' Reckoning
calendars.users: 'most of the users of the Westron language, except the Hobbits'

special.note: New Year — holiday
spring-day, between Súlimë and Víressë — holiday
mid-year's day — holiday
autumn-day, between Yavannië and Narquelië — holiday
last day — holiday

calendars.leap: App D does not restate the leap rule; continuation of the Kings' rule (two enderi for loëndë every fourth year save the haranyë) is inferred. [I-H]
calendars.epoch: Issued by Mardil to take effect in T.A. 2060, after a special addition of 2 days to 2059 (concluding 5½ millennia of the system). Hador added 1 day in T.A. 2360; after that no more adjustments. By the end of the Third Age the Deficit had not yet amounted to 1 day.
notes: All twelve months of 30 days; the five out-of-month days were holidays.

calendars.name: New Reckoning
calendars.users: all the lands of King Elessar, except the Shire

special.note: = March 25 old style, in commemoration of the fall of Sauron and the deeds of the Ring-bearers
days 182–184, between Yavannië and Narquelië; the second was Loëndë; = September 23–25 old style
Yavannië 30 = old September 22, Frodo's birthday, made a festival; doubled in leap-years
last day

calendars.leap: The leap-year was provided for by doubling the feast of Cormarë. [C]
calendars.epoch: Began in the restored Kingdom in T.A. 3019: a return to Kings' Reckoning adapted to a spring-beginning as in the Eldarin loa. Fourth Age 1 = the year beginning March 25, 3021 old style (though the Age was held to begin with Elrond's departure, September 3021; the Shire called that year 1422).
notes: Months kept their Quenya names but now begin with Víressë (April), 'referred to periods beginning generally five days earlier than previously.'
The Imladris yestarë fell later: 'it corresponded more or less with Shire April 6.'

calendars.name: Calendar of Imladris (Reckoning of Rivendell)
calendars.era: recorded for the Third Age; Eldarin in origin
calendars.users: the Elves of Rivendell; 'the Hobbits only provide information concerning the Calendar of Imladris'

week.note: A six-day week (enquië), 'for ritual rather than practical purposes', dedicated to the Stars, Sun, Moon, Two Trees, Heavens, and the Valar — the last the chief day. The yén contained 8,766 enquier, reckoned continuously.

special.note: first day, immediately before tuilë; corresponded more or less with Shire April 6 ('the Elves' New Year')
middle-days between yávië and quellë (days 182–184 of the loa) Corrected: the enderi fall between yávië and quellë, days 182–184; 128 is the first day of yávië.
last day, immediately after coirë

calendars.leap: The enderi were doubled (3 days added) every twelfth year. In the Reckoning of Rivendell the last year of every third yén was shortened by three days: the doubling due that year was omitted — 'but that has not happened in our time.' Of any remaining inaccuracy there is no record. [C]
calendars.epoch: Long periods: the yén = 144 solar years = 52,596 days. The solar year: coranar ('sun-round') or loa ('growth'). A day, ré, ran from sunset to sunset.
calendars.precision: richly documented (the one Elvish calendar the Red Book records)
notes: Six long 'seasons' rather than months; lairë and hrívë had 72 days, the rest 54.
Season-schemes 'no doubt varied in different regions' — only Imladris is recorded. [C]
Twilights held special names: tindómë (near dawn) and undómë (evening); Sindarin uial, minuial/aduial; Shire morrowdim and evendim.

calendars.name: Valian Years (the Reckoning of the Trees)
calendars.era: Years of the Trees, before the Sun and Moon
calendars.users: the Valar and the Eldar of Aman; the Annals of Aman are dated in Valian Years
calendars.epoch: Three schemes across the corpus (displayed, not resolved — see the contradiction register): (1) the earliest Annals: 1 V.Y. = 10 sun-years; (2) the Annals of Aman: the Valian Day of 12 Tree-hours (each = 7 of our hours), the Valian Year of 1000 Valian Days, whence 1 V.Y. = 84,000 hours = 9.582 sun-years; (3) the late 'Time and Ageing' texts (c. 1957 on): 1 V.Y. = 144 sun-years = the Elvish yén, with 12 Valian Months of 12 Valian Days, each Valian Day = 1 löa.
calendars.precision: fragmentary and self-contradictory by design of the drafts
calendars.conf: C (each scheme); the choice between them is an unresolved draft evolution
notes: AAm: after a thousand days the Trees put out a new branch — 'the Valian year of 1000 days was also due to the Trees' nature.'
AAm arithmetic: the Trees first flowered after 3500 V.Y.; the Moon first rose after 5000 V.Y.; the Trees shone 1495 V.Y.
NoME: 'There are twelve Tree-hours in each Valian Day, 144 Days in each Valian Year. But each Valian Year = 144 Mortal Years.'
The Archive's timeline uses the AAm ratio (×9.582) for Valian-dated annals — an adopted default, with the alternatives kept visible.

calendars.name: The Dwarves' Year (Durin's Day)
calendars.era: attested for the late Third Age

special.note: 'The first day of the dwarves' New Year is as all should know the first day of the last moon of Autumn on the threshold of Winter. We still call it Durin's Day when the last moon of Autumn and the sun are in the sky together.'

calendars.epoch: A Moon-governed New Year. Beyond Thorin's rule the corpus gives no month-scheme, no week, and no year-count for the Dwarves.
calendars.conf: C (the rule); everything further: the attached documents do not provide enough information to answer this.
notes: Thorin: 'we cannot guess when such a time will come again' — even the Dwarves could not always predict it.
cites: The Hobbit, ch. III 'A Short Rest'

calendars.name: Bree Reckoning
calendars.users: the Men and Hobbits of Bree (month-names also in the Eastfarthing)

week.note: As the Shire, whose reform Bree eventually adopted.

special.note: Bree's name for the Lithedays
Bree kept the Shire Reckoning under other names; the Yuledays were missing and the year summed to 363.
Bree kept the Shire Reckoning under other names; the Yuledays were missing and the year summed to 363.

calendars.leap: As the Shire — but T.A. 1300 'was the first year of the century' in Bree's count. [C]
calendars.precision: documented as a named variant of Shire Reckoning
notes: A Bree jest spoke of 'Winterfilth in the (muddy) Shire'; the Shire-folk answered that Wintring was the Bree alteration.

units.d: The Númenórean pace, 'slightly longer than our yard, approximately thirty-eight inches, owing to their greater stature.' A full stride 'might be well nigh a ranga and a half.'

'Five thousand rangar (full paces) made a lár, which was very nearly three of our miles' — 5,277 yards, 2 feet and 4 inches, 'supposing the equivalence to be exact.' Lár meant 'pause': a brief halt was usually made after this distance. Númenórean reckoning was decimal.
The longest measure of distance used in the text; 'one league is generally considered equal to about three miles.'

Sam's Lórien rope: ''twenty, thirty ells, more or less' … 'Thirty ells, or say, about eighteen fathom'' — the text's own conversion: 1 ell = 0.6 fathom = 3.6 ft. (The traditional English ell of 45 in differs — the hobbit ell here is the cloth-ell of 3½–3¾ ft range.)
Used throughout the narrative and on the maps as translations of Westron measures; the Companion notes league-scale distances against the maps ('Three hundred leagues is 900 miles').
'Two rangar was often called man-high, which at thirty-eight inches gives an average height of six feet four inches' — a conventional unit of the later Dúnedain, not an observed average.
'More than man-high by nearly half a ranga' — the tallest of all who escaped the Downfall, called Elendil the Tall.
'The tallest of all the women of the Eldar of whom tales tell', said to be man-high 'according to the measure of the Dúnedain and the men of old.'
'The Hobbits of the Shire were in height between three and four feet, never less and seldom more' — the Númenórean name Halfling 'was approximately accurate when given.'

'In Gondor tharni was used for a silver coin, the fourth part of the castar (in Noldorin the canath or fourth part of the mirian).' Tharni was also the old word for 'quarter' rendered as farthing in the Shire's divisions.
units.modern: no real-world value stated — the attached documents do not provide enough information to answer this.

units.d: 'Bill Ferny's price was twelve silver pennies; and that was indeed at least three times the pony's value.' Barliman gave Merry 'eighteen pence as some compensation', and reckoned his whole loss at thirty silver pennies — 'a sore blow to him.'
units.rel: a pony ≈ 4 silver pennies (fair price, computed from 12 = 3× value)
units.conf: C (the prices); I-H (the pony's fair price)

units.d: 'A day of the sun they called ré and reckoned from sunset to sunset' — against the Númenórean day from sunrise to sunrise.
units.rel: 365 rér ≈ 1 loa; the yén contained 52,596 days

units.d: The ritual six-day week; 8,766 enquier in a yén, reckoned continuously — no intercalary day ever broke the sequence.
The solar year: coranar 'sun-round' astronomically, loa 'growth' seasonally. Appendix D notes the year's true length: 365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes, 46 seconds.
'The Quenya word yén, often translated year, really means 144 of our years. The Eldar preferred to reckon in sixes and twelves as far as possible.'
The last year of a century of the Númenórean system, in which the leap-doubling was omitted.

'The Valian day had twelve hours', each Tree-hour = 7 of our hours, the day therefore 84 hours; 1000 Valian Days = 1 Valian Year = 84,000 hours = 9.582 sun-years. The day's length was 'of the Trees' nature': the period of mingled light was five times shorter than a Tree's full flowering.
units.modern: Tree-hour = 7 h; Valian Day = 3.5 solar days

units.d: NoME: '1 Valian Day (or Tree-day) = 1 Sun-year'; 12 Valian Days = 1 Valian Month; 12 Valian Months = 1 Valian Year = 144 sun-years = 1 yén.
Minas Tirith rang its hours from sunrise: 'the third hour from the rising of the sun', 'ere the second hour from noon' — bell-marked day-hours in the Númenórean manner.

Old Entish was 'a lovely language, but it takes a very long time to say anything in it, because we do not say anything in it, unless it is worth taking a long time to say.' Treebeard's unhastiness is a manner of time-keeping in itself: 'Do not be hasty, that is my motto.'
Not a coin but a contract: Bilbo's 'one fourteenth share of total profits (if any)' of the treasure of Erebor — hobbit-lawyerly precision applied to a dragon-hoard.
units.rel: 1/14 of the hoard 'in silver and gold, setting aside the gems'

units.d: Appendix D's own metrology: the millennial deficit of the Kings' Reckoning was 4 hours, 46 minutes, 40 seconds; the exiles' dislocations left 'about 8 hours' for Mardil to mend; 'by the end of the Third Age, after 660 more years, the Deficit had not yet amounted to 1 day.'

festivals.when: the last three and first three days of each year
festivals.note: The Yuledays (1 Yule, 2 Yule) were chief holidays and times of feasting; Yuletide in full was six days.

festivals.when: 1 Lithe, Mid-year's Day, 2 Lithe (and the Overlithe in leap-years)
festivals.note: Midsummer feasting; the Overlithe 'a day of special merrymaking' — the great one fell in 1420.

festivals.when: the White Downs at the Lithe
festivals.note: 'At the Free Fair on the White Downs at the Lithe, that is at Midsummer' the Mayor of Michel Delving was elected for seven years.

'A custom of making holiday and dancing in the Party Field, when weather permitted, on April 6' — Sam's birthday to some, the flowering of the Golden Tree in 1420 to others, the Elves' New Year to others still.
'The Horn of the Mark was blown at sundown every November 2 and bonfires and feastings followed' — anniversary of its first blowing in 3019.
New Year 'in commemoration of the fall of Sauron and the deeds of the Ring-bearers.'
Frodo's birthday made a festival in his honour.

festivals.n: The five holidays of the Stewards
festivals.note: The days outside the months were holidays throughout the Westron lands.
'Prayer for the coming year' — the King ascended the Meneltarma; the Three Prayers were the only appointed worship of Eru in Númenor.
festivals.cite: UT, 'A Description of the Island of Númenor'

festivals.note: 'Praise of Eru Ilúvatar' upon the Meneltarma.
festivals.cite: UT, 'A Description of the Island of Númenor'

festivals.note: 'Thanksgiving to Eru' — the autumn feast of Númenor.
festivals.cite: UT, 'A Description of the Island of Númenor'

festivals.note: 'The festival of Nost-na-Lothion or the Birth of Flowers', kept 'with revelry of children' in Gondolin.

festivals.n: Tarnin Austa — the Gates of Summer
festivals.note: A solemn vigil from midnight to dawn, 'and no voice was uttered' until the dawn-song — but that year the red light rose in the north: the city fell at the feast.

festivals.n: Mereth Aderthad — the Feast of Reuniting
festivals.when: F.A. 20, near the pools of Ivrin
festivals.note: Held by Fingolfin in the Year of the Sun 20; 'many counsels were taken in good will.'

festivals.when: first day of the last moon of Autumn, sun and moon in the sky together
festivals.note: The Dwarves' New Year. Its fall cannot be computed from the corpus: the attached documents do not provide enough information to answer this.
'We look towards Númenor that was, and beyond to Elvenhome that is, and to that which is beyond Elvenhome and will ever be.'

anchors.rule: Day-of-year identity: both begin at mid-winter, both hold 365 days, and “The date was, however, March 25 in both Kings' and Stewards' Reckoning” at the fall of Barad-dûr (= Shire Rethe 25, day 86).
anchors.conf: C at the anchor; I-H generalized (month-lengths differ within the year, and the two systems' leap-days and historical Deficit adjustments drifted by hours, not whole days, by the end of the Third Age)

anchors.rule: N.R. yestarë = March 25 old style (Shire day 86); the 3 Enderi = old September 23–25; Cormarë (Yavannië 30) = old September 22 — all three anchors are consistent with a constant offset of 85 days against the Shire year.

The Imladris yestarë 'corresponded more or less with Shire April 6' (day 97). The Imladris leap-cycle (12-year enderi-doubling) does not track the Shire's 4-year rule, so the alignment wanders by up to 3 days within a cycle.

'March 25… would correspond to our March 27, if our years began at the same seasonal point' — i.e. equal day-of-year: Shire day 86 = our 27 March (day 86). Differences arise only from month-shapes (Shire February has 30 days, October 30, etc.).

'Mid-year's Day was intended to correspond as nearly as possible to the summer solstice. In that case the Shire dates were actually in advance of ours by some ten days, and our New Year's Day corresponded more or less to the Shire January 9' — seasonally, our day-of-year ≈ Shire day-of-year − 9.
anchors.conf: I-M — Tolkien's 'as nearly as possible' and 'more or less'

anchors.rule: S.R. year = T.A. − 1600 · Bree year = T.A. − 1299 · T.A. 1 = S.A. 3442 · Fourth Age 1 = T.A. 3021 (N.R.; the Shire called it 1422) · Kings' count began S.A. 1.

Three corpus schemes: ×10 (earliest Annals), ×9.582 (Annals of Aman), ×144 (late NoME texts, = yén). No single canonical ratio exists; the Archive defaults to ×9.582 for AAm-dated annals and shows all three.
anchors.conf: C for each scheme; the selection is an editorial default

limits: Exact day-alignment across millennia (through the Deficit corrections, the 441-year-late additions, Mardil's 2 days and Hador's 1) is stated by Tolkien only in summary; conversions here are exact within a year's structure and honest about drift across eras.
No mapping of any Middle-earth date to a real historical year exists in the corpus: Gregorian equivalences are seasonal/structural only.

note: All five real calendars balance to 365 days and the conversion block satisfies three of Tolkien's independent equations simultaneously. New Reckoning leap-years double Cormarë, making mettarë day 366; the tables here show the common year.
